critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Early-spring browser feeding hard as snow fades and fresh forage slowly returns.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer use their white tail as a flag to signal danger to other deer.
critters.activeFact: As fields and trail edges open up, deer spend more time browsing fresh growth at dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Large forest mammal beginning to reappear as spring conditions improve.
critters.fact: Black bears have excellent climbing skills and use trees to escape danger or find food.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can bring the season’s earliest bear movement, especially during milder stretches.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Active spring predator traveling field edges, trails, and thawing open ground.
critters.fact: Coyotes use playful howls and yips to communicate and strengthen social bonds within their pack.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, coyotes often work open edges and damp low ground where scent and sound carry well.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Solitary hunter working patchy fields and woodland edges in early spring.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance and to keep warm while resting in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: As the first week of spring opens up more ground, red foxes often become easier to spot in open country.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Secretive woodland fox moving through brushy cover during the spring transition.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are excellent climbers, often climbing trees to escape predators or find food.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, gray foxes may shift activity with the warming days and still-cold nights.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Elusive predator still hunting actively through the muddy spring transition.
critters.fact: Bobcats are solitary hunters with excellent night vision, allowing them to stalk prey quietly in the dark.
critters.activeFact: As snow shrinks and trails soften, bobcats often travel wooded margins where prey activity begins increasing.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-moving tree feeder still relying on bark, buds, and early spring browse.
critters.fact: Porcupines cannot shoot their quills; quills detach only when predators make contact.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring still finds porcupines focused on woody foods while fresh vegetation slowly wakes up.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Wetland builder becoming more active as ponds and creeks open up.
critters.fact: Beavers build dams using sticks, mud, and rocks to create safe aquatic homes called lodges.
critters.activeFact: As open water expands in the first week of spring, beavers often show fresh shoreline and lodge activity.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Aquatic rodent more visible as thaw opens shorelines and marsh edges.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from vegetation, often in wetlands, using sticks and mud.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, muskrats often cruise open water pockets and shoreline runs for fresh plant material.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic hunter working open banks and thawed water edges.
critters.fact: Minks have waterproof fur that keeps them dry and warm while hunting in water.
critters.activeFact: As more water opens in early spring, mink often patrol banks where fish, frogs, and small mammals are exposed.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Forest carnivore ranging widely through timber during the spring shift.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and use their agility to hunt prey in trees and on the ground.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring often finds fishers moving through ridges and coverts as the woods begin loosening up.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Aquatic predator enjoying the return of open water in early spring.
critters.fact: River otters can close their ears and nostrils underwater to stay dry while swimming.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, otters often use melting banks and open leads to move, hunt, and play.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Tree squirrel busy with feeding and nest activity as spring settles in.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels can rotate their hind feet 180 degrees to climb down trees head-first.
critters.activeFact: Milder spring days often bring more chasing, calling, and nest-building traffic through the treetops.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Territorial woodland squirrel defending food stores into early spring.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their territory using loud, high-pitched vocalizations to warn intruders.
critters.activeFact: As ground opens and cone stores shrink, red squirrels often get even louder around favorite stash spots.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Ground squirrel appearing more often as early spring warmth returns.
critters.fact: Chipmunks have cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can bring quick chipmunk dashes around logs, woodpiles, and burrow entrances.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Forest hare entering the awkward seasonal shift between snow and bare ground.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally, turning white in winter for camouflage in snow.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can leave snowshoe hares standing out more as camouflage struggles to keep up.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Common rabbit feeding along brushy openings and emerging spring edges.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails use their strong hind legs to make quick, zigzag escapes from predators.
critters.activeFact: As the first week of spring opens more feeding lanes, cottontails often work hedges, yards, and field margins.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Nocturnal forager becoming more active with longer, milder spring nights.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws that help them open containers and find food in the dark.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, raccoons may range more widely around woods, water, and easy feeding spots.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive marsupial more visible as early spring nights moderate.
critters.fact: Opossums play dead as a defense, dropping their body temperature and secretions to appear lifeless.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring often brings more opossum movement around brush, yards, and leftover food sources.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Night forager especially active during the spring breeding window.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a powerful, foul-smelling liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is prime time for extra skunk movement around fields, edges, and thawed ground.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Gliding nocturnal squirrel staying active through cool spring nights.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide up to 150 feet using a stretchy membrane between their limbs called a patagium.
critters.activeFact: Even in the first week of spring, flying squirrels stay mostly hidden by day and busy overhead after dark.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Insect-eating bat waiting on warmer spring nights and more bugs.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects mid-flight with incredible precision.
critters.activeFact: A mild first-week-of-spring evening can trigger limited little brown bat activity near roosts and water.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Hardy bat capable of taking advantage of early spring warm spells.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats can eat up to half their body weight in insects each night, boosting pest control naturally.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, a soft warm night may bring big brown bats out near buildings and lights.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Woodland owl vocal through the shift from late winter into true spring.
critters.fact: Barred Owls can rotate their heads nearly 270 degrees to spot prey without moving their bodies.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is a great time to hear barred owls calling after sunset and before dawn.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful owl often already deep into nesting by early spring.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls have specialized feathers that enable nearly silent flight to surprise their prey.
critters.activeFact: By the first week of spring, great horned owls may already be well into nesting season despite chilly nights.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Bold songbird getting louder and more territorial in early spring.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic the calls of hawks to scare away other birds from feeders.
critters.activeFact: Early spring blue jays often call hard, chase rivals, and act more serious about feeder territory.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Bright red songbird fully leaning into spring singing season.
critters.fact: Northern Cardinals sing at night during bright moonlight to defend their territory.
critters.activeFact: A cardinal singing on a cold first-week-of-spring morning is one of the clearest signs the season has turned.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Energetic feeder bird moving into pair-bonding and nest-checking season.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of hiding spots for their food throughout winter.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, chickadees often mix quick feeding with more calling, chasing, and pairing behavior.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious woodland songbird growing more vocal as nesting season nears.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice are known for their clear, whistled "peter-peter-peter" song used for communication.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring often brings titmice to exposed perches where they call out across the woods.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Tree-climbing bird active on bark and early spring territory routes.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches can walk headfirst down tree trunks to find hidden insects.
critters.activeFact: As the first week of spring settles in, nuthatches often get more vocal while still hammering seeds and bark crevices.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small woodpecker drumming more as spring territory season strengthens.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers use their stiff tail feathers to brace against trees while pecking for insects.
critters.activeFact: That quick tapping in the first week of spring often means a downy woodpecker is making itself known.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Large woodland woodpecker growing louder with spring breeding behavior.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long beaks to drill deep into trees searching for insects.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, hairy woodpeckers often work larger trunks and advertise territory with sharp drumming.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Big forest woodpecker becoming more obvious as spring territory activity ramps up.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large rectangular holes in trees to find insects and create nesting sites.
critters.activeFact: A deep hammering burst in the first week of spring can be a pileated woodpecker announcing the season.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Common woodland woodpecker more active and vocal in early spring.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers can store acorns in tree bark for winter food.
critters.activeFact: Early spring red-bellied woodpeckers may spend more time chasing rivals and less time quietly feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Gentle dove easing into courtship behavior as spring gets underway.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour during long-distance migration.
critters.activeFact: By the first week of spring, mourning doves may already be pairing up and choosing early nest spots.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Seasonal finch starting to brighten and wake up with spring energy.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice a year, changing from bright yellow in summer to dull olive in winter.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can bring more lively flock movement and the earliest hints of brighter color.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-season songbird still common during the opening stretch of spring.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos change plumage color slightly with seasons to blend into winter and summer environments.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring often still belongs to juncos under the feeder before later migration changes set in.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Small social finch turning more vocal as spring nesting season nears.
critters.fact: House Finches are known for their vibrant red plumage, which is more intense in males.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, house finches may shift from casual feeding to more active pair behavior and song.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland finch tied to early spring seed and cone availability.
critters.fact: Purple Finches have a sweet, warbling song that varies regionally across North America.
critters.activeFact: Early spring purple finch activity can come in bursts, with quick feeding runs followed by quiet stretches.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Large ground bird shifting from winter flocking into spring display season.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can fly at speeds up to 55 miles per hour in short bursts.
critters.activeFact: By the first week of spring, toms may already be strutting more often even when mornings still feel raw.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest bird entering the opening stretch of spring drumming season.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse can create a booming sound by rapidly beating their wings during courtship displays.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is when that distant engine-like thump in the woods starts becoming more regular.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland bird returning north with the first big wave of spring migration.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform elaborate, synchronized dances to strengthen pair bonds and communicate with each other.
critters.activeFact: A sandhill crane call over thawing wetlands in the first week of spring is a loud seasonal headline.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Smart black bird active across fields, roads, and early spring woodland edges.
critters.fact: American Crows use tools like sticks to extract insects from tree bark for food.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, crows often gather loudly at dawn and patrol openings for food.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large intelligent corvid active over ridges, timber, and opening spring skies.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic sounds from their environment, including human speech and other animal calls.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring may bring ravens soaring, calling, and working together over openings and forest country.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Soaring raptor moving into visible spring nest and territory behavior.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use their keen eyesight to spot prey from over a mile away while soaring high.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, hawks may spend more time soaring and nest-tending than simply hunting.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland hawk entering stronger spring territory behavior.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are skilled fliers, often maneuvering through dense trees to catch their prey mid-air.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, Cooper’s hawks may make sharp aggressive flights while defending nest space.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Common duck back on open water and pairing up for spring.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can drink both fresh and salt water thanks to special glands that filter salt.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring often finds mallards forming pairs and working fresh open water for food.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Colorful cavity-nesting duck returning with early spring wetland activity.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities and have brightly colored males with iridescent plumage.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, wood ducks may show up on flooded timber, bends, and sheltered ponds.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Migratory goose active around thawing water and early nesting areas.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly thousands of miles during migration using a distinctive V-formation for energy efficiency.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can make geese extra noisy and territorial around ponds, marshes, and open fields.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Large wading bird returning as streams, marshes, and pond edges open.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons can stand still for minutes, patiently hunting fish with their sharp, spear-like beaks.
critters.activeFact: A heron beside a partly thawed stream in the first week of spring is one of the season’s quieter comeback shots.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Basking turtle waiting for the first reliable warm-ups of spring.
critters.fact: Painted turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, helping them stay submerged longer.
critters.activeFact: A warm first-week-of-spring afternoon can put painted turtles on display even while nights still dip cold.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Large aquatic turtle still mostly quiet but waking slowly with spring.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles can snap their jaws shut in less than 0.1 seconds, making them fierce predators.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring may start the slow shift from winter stillness toward more snapping turtle movement.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Panfish beginning to react to longer days and slightly warming shallows.
critters.fact: Bluegills can recognize individual humans and remember them for months.
critters.activeFact: During the first week of spring, bluegill often hold near cover where slightly warmer water and food gather.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Freshwater predator easing toward spring staging behavior.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can grow over 20 inches long and use their large mouths to ambush prey.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring usually finds bass closer to warming cover, but not fully committed to the shallows yet.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Schooling fish entering one of the earliest spring spawning periods.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have bright yellow bodies with dark vertical stripes, making them easy to identify underwater.
critters.activeFact: Early spring perch may gather in schools near shallower water as spawning approaches or begins.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Fast predator moving into one of its prime early spring periods.
critters.fact: Northern Pike have sharp teeth and can grow over 4 feet long, making them fierce freshwater predators.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is prime time for pike movement toward soft shallow habitat and spawning zones.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny frog preparing to headline the earliest true spring chorus nights.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers are tiny frogs known for their loud, high-pitched peeping calls during early spring nights.
critters.activeFact: A mild first-week-of-spring evening can trigger those first peeper notes, even if frost still shows up by morning.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Large pond frog still waiting for warmer water before really getting going.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce deep, loud calls that carry over long distances to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: In the first week of spring, bullfrogs are often still in slow mode while peepers get the spotlight.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Common shoreline frog beginning to stir as spring warmth builds.
critters.fact: Green frogs can jump over 20 times their body length to escape predators quickly.
critters.activeFact: By the first week of spring, green frogs may begin shifting from winter hiding toward more shoreline activity.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted wetland frog among the earlier amphibians to move in spring.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs get their name from the distinctive dark spots on their green or brown skin.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring may bring leopard frogs out on mild days near open wet ground and thawed pools.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Harmless snake often among the first reptiles spotted each spring.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can swim and are often found near water, hunting amphibians and small fish.
critters.activeFact: A sunny first-week-of-spring afternoon can bring garter snakes out near rocks, boards, and brush piles.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic snake waiting on steadier warmth along spring shorelines.
critters.fact: Water snakes can be mistaken for venomous snakes but are completely harmless and excellent swimmers.
critters.activeFact: During especially sunny early spring days, water snakes may show limited movement near open water edges.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Woodland snake beginning to reappear as spring sunlight reaches shelter spots.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes can mimic venomous snakes by vibrating their tails to scare predators away.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can produce milk snake sightings around barns, woodpiles, and brushy debris on warm afternoons.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Migratory butterfly still mostly south of northern Michigan in the first week of spring.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles between North America and Mexico each year.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is more about monarchs beginning the northbound journey than appearing widely up north.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Large butterfly still waiting on steadier warmth in northern spring country.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies can taste with their feet to find the best leaves for their caterpillars.
critters.activeFact: This stage of spring is still more promise than payoff for swallowtail sightings in the north woods.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Aerial predator still mostly developing underwater this early in spring.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly up to 30 miles per hour, making them some of the fastest insects in the world.
critters.activeFact: Early spring wetlands are full of future dragonflies below the surface long before summer patrol flights begin.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender wetland insect still mostly hidden in aquatic stages during early spring.
critters.fact: Damselflies fold their wings along their body when resting, unlike dragonflies that keep wings open.
critters.activeFact: This early in the season, damselflies are more tucked into pond margins than hovering above them.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer glow insect still dormant during the opening week of spring.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to communicate, especially for attracting mates during warm summer nights.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring is still far too early for blinking firefly nights, even if the woods feel awake again.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Pollinator taking advantage of the first decent spring flying days.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by dancing to share the location of flowers with their hive mates.
critters.activeFact: A sunny early spring afternoon can bring honeybees buzzing out briefly even while mornings still start frosty.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Cold-tolerant bee among the first bold pollinators to reappear in spring.
critters.fact: Bumblebees are excellent pollinators, helping over 90% of wildflower species reproduce.
critters.activeFact: The first week of spring can bring lone queen bumblebees out searching for flowers and nesting spots.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Jumping insect still mostly absent from the first week of spring in northern Michigan.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers have strong hind legs that allow them to jump up to 20 times their body length.
critters.activeFact: This part of spring is still quiet for grasshoppers, with the season only laying the groundwork for later activity.
